description: senior college of the City University of New York
description_wiki: The City College of the City University of New York (also known as the City College of New York, or simply City College or CCNY) is a public research university within the City University of New York (CUNY) system in New York City.

description: American Baptist minister and civil rights leader (1929–1968)

description_wiki: John Dollard (29 August 1900 – 8 October 1980) was an American psychologist and social scientist known for his studies on race relations in America and the frustration-aggression hypothesis he proposed with Neal E. Miller and others.

Mohandas Karamchand Gandhi (; GAHN-dee; 2 October 1869 – 30 January 1948) was an Indian lawyer, anti-colonial nationalist and political ethicist who employed nonviolent resistance to lead the successful campaign for India's independence from British rule, and to later inspire movements for civil rights and freedom across the world.

description: American civil rights activist and minister (1926–1990)
Black nationalist African-American new religious movement
description_wiki: The Nation of Islam (NOI) is a religious organization founded in the United States by Wallace Fard Muhammad in 1930.

description: state of the United States of America
description_wiki: Mississippi (  MISS-iss-IP-ee) is a state in the Southeastern and Deep South regions of the United States.
Tampa (  TAM-pə) is a major city on the Gulf Coast of the U.S. state of Florida and the county seat of Hillsborough County.
Medgar Wiley Evers (; July 2, 1925 – June 12, 1963) was an American civil rights activist who was the NAACP's first field secretary in Mississippi.

description: American Civil Rights Campaign in Alabama
description_wiki: The Birmingham campaign, also known as the Birmingham movement or Birmingham confrontation, was an American movement organized in early 1963 by the Southern Christian Leadership Conference  to bring attention to the integration efforts of African Americans in Birmingham, Alabama.

The White Citizens' Councils were an associated network of white supremacist, segregationist organizations in the United States, concentrated in the South and created as part of a white backlash against the US Supreme Court's landmark Brown v.
